content.full_text:
STATE OF CAT ORNIA
PEIE WILSON, Go€rnor
.PUBLIC UTILITIES COMMISSION
505 VAN NESS AVENUE
sAN FRANCTSCO, CA 9,(102-3298
AUO l
F
'esz
Aug'ust 7 , 1992
JiIl 
Stecher
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804-0814
Dear Ms. Stecher:
Res Request for Advice No. A-92-346
The purpose of this letter is to get further cl-arification of
fornal, written advice I received from you on Jul,y 24, 1992. (See
Attachment A, Request for Advice No. A-92-346. )
You letter indicated that the CPUC'S reinbursement for travel
expenses incurred by Timothy Kenney and l{ark Bumgardner in
connection with an inspection of the books and records of Pacific
cas and E.l,ectric Company's (PG&E's) Canadian subsidiaries and
affiliates 
would not be a gift or income to the employees, even
if the CPUC is, in turn, reimbursed by PG&E for the travel
exPenses .
Mr. Bumgardner has received a letter fron PG&E'S attorneys in
response to your letter. 
(See Attachment B, Letter from Chery1
white Mason to Mark Bumgardner, dated August 3, 1992.) PG&E
cites In re Stone (L971) 3 FPPC 52,57, which sets forth the
criteria for determining whether there has been a gift to the
state and not to the official . 
PG&E asserts that unl-ess the CPUC
adopts a Stone resolution, thereby demonstrating conpliance with
the requirements of the Fair Pol-itical- Practices Comrnission
(FPPC), PG&E will not reimburse the CPUC for the travel expenses.
PG&E further contends that A-92-354 does not address the "key
issue, " which is whether PG&E'S reimbursement of the travel
expenses would be a gift to the individual CPUC employees, Mr.
Kenney and Mr. Bumgardner.
Although I believe that this issue is addressed implicitly in A-
92-364, I would appreciate your clarification 
of this issue.
Specifically, I am requesting that you address the following
issues raised by PG&E. First, is PG&E'S reimbursement to the
CPUC a gift to the individual employees who will be traveling to
Canada to conduct the inspection? Second, must acceptance of
PG&E 's reimbursement comply with the requirements of -I-OIC-..]9!9!C,
including the adoption of a Stone resol-ution, j-n order to prevent
PG&E's reimbursement from being a gift to the individual
employees .


Jill stecher
August 7, L992
P age 2
As set forth in my original letter to you, I believe that
acceptance of PG&E's reimbursement meets the criteria set forth
in In re Stone for a gift to the state, except for the adoption
of a lesolution. (See Attachment C, Letter fron Judith A1len to
the FPPC, dated June L9, 1992.1 Although the CPUC could adopt
such a resolution in this case, this is a recurring situation.
The CPUC is often reimbursed by public utilities 
for the costs of
travel associated with audits and inspections. Therefore, it
would be burdensome to adopt a resolution in each case if it is
not necessary in order to comply with the Political Reform Act.
It should be noted that al,though my original letter to you stated
that the trip in question was to occur on Jurte 22, 1992, it has
now been postponed until- Septenber of 1992. Therefore, this
request does not relate to past conduct.
Please call me at (415) 703-2053 if you need any further
information regarding this request for advice.
S incerely,
ct- A
AlIen
Public Util,ities Counsef
JA s afm
Attachments

sections.question: First, is PG&E's reimbursement to the CPUC a gift to the individual employees who will be traveling to Canada to conduct the inspection? Second, must acceptance of PG&E's reimbursement comply with the requirements of the Political Reform Act, including the adoption of a Stone resolution, in order to prevent PG&E's reimbursement from being a gift to the individual employees.
sections.question_synthetic: The CPUC seeks clarification on whether PG&E's reimbursement of travel expenses for CPUC employees to inspect PG&E's Canadian subsidiaries constitutes a gift to the individual employees, and whether adoption of a Stone resolution is required to comply with the Political Reform Act.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from CPUC to FPPC, not an FPPC response. The document references a prior FPPC advice letter (A-92-346) dated July 24, 1992 and raises follow-up questions prompted by PG&E's response citing In re Stone precedent. No formal conclusion section present as this is a request document, not a response.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: First, is PG&E's reimbursement to the CPUC a gift to the individual employees who will be traveling to Canada to conduct the inspection? Second, must acceptance of PG&E's reimbursement comply with the requirements of the Political Reform Act, including the adoption of a Stone resolution, in order to prevent PG&E's reimbursement from being a gift to the individual employees.

embedding.summary: This is a follow-up request from the California Public Utilities Commission seeking clarification from the FPPC regarding a previous advice letter (A-92-346) about whether PG&E's reimbursement of travel expenses for CPUC employees constitutes a gift to those employees under the Political Reform Act.
